cards.text: A null pointer is initialized using the keyword {{c1::nullptr}}: {{c2::int* ptr = nullptr;}}. It means the pointer is not pointing to anything.

cards.front: What happens when you dereference a null pointer?
cards.back: Dereferencing a null pointer leads to undefined behavior, typically causing a program crash. Always check if a pointer is null before dereferencing.

cards.question: How do you safely check if a pointer is null?
cards.answer: Use if (ptr) or if (ptr != nullptr). Pointers convert to bool: nullptr is false, non-null is true.
cards.explanation: Always check pointers before dereferencing to avoid undefined behavior.

cards.text: Use {{c1::nullptr}} instead of 0 or NULL for null pointers in modern C++. It's type-safe and unambiguous.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Dereferencing a null pointer causes undefined behavior. Must assign ptr to a valid address before dereferencing.
cards.explanation: A null pointer doesn't point to any valid memory location, so dereferencing it is dangerous.

cards.front: When should you initialize a pointer to nullptr?
cards.back: Initialize a pointer to nullptr when you don't have a valid object to point to yet. This makes it safe to check if the pointer is valid before using it.

cards.explanation:
Follow these guidelines for null pointers:
  • Use nullptr, not 0 or NULL
  • Initialize pointers to nullptr if no valid address yet
  • Always check before dereferencing
  • Dereferencing nullptr is undefined behavior
  • Pointers convert to bool (nullptr = false)
examples: int* ptr = nullptr;  // Initialize to null

cards.text: A pointer can be checked with {{c1::if (ptr)}} because pointers convert to {{c2::bool}}, where nullptr is {{c3::false}}.
